required_complexity_patterns.notes: When combobox widget requested, use custom listbox - native select alone is insufficient
Label wraps input through nested divs without for attribute
Apply form hosted inside iframe or srcdoc iframe shell
Newsletter or login decoy form separate from job application form
Field labeled via aria-labelledby pointing at hidden or distant span

SVG icon labels

Full name

prompt_summary: Create a messy, production-real ashby-style job application (single-page, large, tier=high). Widgets: combobox. Required: When combobox widget requested, use custom listbox - native select alone is insufficient Required: Label wraps input through nested divs without for attribute Required: Apply form hosted inside iframe or srcdoc iframe shell Required: Newsletter or login decoy form separate from job application form Required: Field labeled via aria-labelledby pointing at hidden or distant span Reference DOM (Visibility hidden input): Input uses visibility:hidden instead of display:none Reference DOM (Duplicate element IDs): Two inputs share id="email" - tests disambiguation by name or DOM order Reference DOM (SVG icon labels): Label contains SVG icon; accessible name via aria-labelledby only Do NOT produce a tutorial-style form where every field is label[for]+native input. Mix native controls with non-semantic production DOM from the patterns below. Implement ALL required complexity patterns: native-select-not-combobox, label-wraps-deep, iframe-apply, decoy-form, aria-labelledby-hidden. Include at least one custom widget (div role=textbox, pill buttons, or listbox combobox). Include at least one labeling strategy besides label-for (aria-label, aria-labelledby, glued spans, placeholder-only). Layout: definition list dl/dt/dd label pairs. Avoid generic centered SaaS card as the only structure. High tier: include shadow DOM host OR iframe apply shell OR wizard/conditional reveal with hidden fields. Mirror structural messiness like these references: Visibility hidden input; Duplicate element IDs; SVG icon labels.
